main_summary.summary: Carrie Lamar Young, interviewed September 26, 2011 at her home in Little Rock, Arkansas by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program, recounts her trajectory from sharecropping childhood in Lexa and Barton, Arkansas (Phillips County) to SNCC organizing in Helena and West Helena, Arkansas beginning in 1965. Born to Lazarus and Lottie Lamar, she describes Delta plantation labor, her mother's midwifery work, and the Lamar family's renaming from the Raymond plantation. She joined SNCC's Arkansas Project after Howard Himmelbaum, Bill Hansen, Jim Jones, and Jerry Casey arrived in West Helena, Arkansas, gathering signatures to abolish the poll tax, teaching literacy so people could register to vote, and integrating the Midland Heights bowling alley with Stanley Tillman before the Civil Rights Act passed. Young married Himmelbaum in March 1967, lived through interracial-marriage hostility, won class-action employment lawsuits against AMF Inc and National Old Line Life Insurance, helped elect Winthrop Rockefeller, co-founded the Black United Youth in Little Rock, and was in Memphis in 1968 supporting Dr. King. She later attended Malcolm X Liberation University in Durham in 1971.
key_themes: Poll tax abolition and voter education
Interracial marriage in the segregated South
Phillips County and the Elaine Massacre legacy
Black United Youth and post-SNCC activism
main_summary.historical_significance:
Young's interview is one of the few first-person accounts of SNCC's Arkansas Project from a Helena/West Helena, Arkansas-based local organizer rather than from one of the visiting field secretaries. She names specific Arkansas SNCC personnel (Howard Himmelbaum, Bill Hansen, Jim Jones, Jerry Casey, James Heel, Stanley Tillman, Michael Simmons, Myrtle Glasgow) and reconstructs the practical work of poll-tax-abolition signature drives and literacy classes that preceded the 1965 Voting Rights Act in the Arkansas Delta. Her account of Phillips County children walking on cracked earth and being told the hardness came from blood preserves an oral transmission of the 1919 Elaine Massacre memory to a generation born decades later. Her testimony on the founding of the Black United Youth in Little Rock, the 1968 desegregation confrontation in Earle, Arkansas, and the role of John W. Walker in Young v. Lozano provides specific organizational and legal history of post-SNCC Arkansas movement work.

The interview also documents the personal cost of interracial activist marriage in 1960s-70s Arkansas and Young's reflections, in 2011, on the limits she perceived in the movement's economic outcomes and on Barack Obama's election.

chapters.title: A Welcome in Little Rock
chapters.summary: Joe Mosnier opens the September 2011 interview at Carrie Lamar Young's Little Rock home, and she begins with her parents Lazarus and Lottie Lamar and her place as the youngest of four girls in a family of six children.

chapters.title: Sharecropping in Lexa
chapters.summary: She describes growing up sharecropping in Lexa, raising the family's own gardens and livestock and working the cotton fields, riding the train into Helena to shop, and a father who reached only the third grade yet was sharp with numbers.

chapters.title: A Midwife Mother and a Family Renamed
chapters.summary: Young recalls her mother, a skilled midwife who could turn a breach baby and who concealed that she could not read until Young was twenty-three, and the family history an uncle shared, including the plantation name Raymond that a great-great-grandfather changed to Lamar.

chapters.title: A Grandfather Who Whittled, and the Shotgun House
chapters.summary: She remembers a grandfather who lived to one hundred four and whittled rings and bamboo flutes for the children, the triplex shotgun house they shared with two other families, and the communal hog slaughters and crackling-making of a deeply rural life.

chapters.title: School Between the Harvests
chapters.summary: Young describes a schooling pulled apart by the harvest, with backless hand-me-down books from the white schools, and how being the youngest girl gave her a head start and spared her some of the field work her older siblings lost school to.

chapters.title: Learning Jim Crow, and a Father's Armed Watch
chapters.summary: She recalls learning to navigate Jim Crow by watching her parents and by her mother's counsel never to fear asking for what she needed, and the night her father slipped out with a gun to join men guarding the road to protect a young Black man a white merchant's daughter had accused.

chapters.title: The Girl in the Cotton Wagon
chapters.summary: Young tells of a white girl her own age who taunted her in the cotton wagon until Young nearly threw her out, stopped only by her father passing by, and of running into the same woman decades later at a Western Union counter, where Young made her serve the waiting Black customers.

chapters.title: The Other Side of the Tracks
chapters.summary: She maps Lexa's racial geography around the railroad tracks, the Black side with its train station, cafe, and garage, and the Italian and German farming families like the Muscalinos that her family chose to work with.

chapters.title: Picking Cotton, and the Weighing Scale
chapters.summary: Young describes a childhood of picking and chopping cotton from sunup to sundown, the whippings under the weighing scale when she fell short of a hundred pounds, the kind landowner Homer Tiles who would beg her mother to spare her, and her father's work at the ice plant and gin.

chapters.title: Church, Singing, and Salvation
chapters.summary: She recalls her love of church and singing at First Baptist in Lexa, the revival pastor who had her sing, Miss Molly Richardson's Saturday sunshine band, and a Christian experience she only fully understood decades later.

chapters.title: The Blood in the Earth at Elaine
chapters.summary: Young recounts working the hard, cracked Phillips County ground as a teenager and asking why it was so hard, until the elders told the children about the 1919 Elaine massacre and the blood they said was in the earth.

chapters.title: A Sister Lost, and the Colorism of School
chapters.summary: She traces her determination to leave to a sister who died studying at Columbia, a loss that broke her own pursuit of higher education, and to the colorism in Black schools that discriminated by skin tone and family, which she fought through in her talents for singing, spelling, and the stage.

chapters.title: SNCC Comes to West Helena
chapters.summary: Young recalls SNCC organizers arriving, Howard Himmelbaum, Jim Jones, Bill Hansen, who met with local churches around voter education and abolishing the poll tax, and how she, one of the few girls to step up, got her parents' permission to work with them.

chapters.title: The White Backlash
chapters.summary: She describes the white reprisals against the civil rights workers, Molotov cocktails thrown at their homes, arrests for vagrancy, while figures like Julian Bond, Andrew Young, Stokely Carmichael, and Marion Barry came through for multi-day church meetings.

chapters.title: The Poll Tax Petition
chapters.summary: Young recounts the work she and Howard Himmelbaum took on once they realized people could not read or write: teaching them to read and at least sign their names in a church basement so they could register and meet the new application requirements, against the threat of the vigilantes.

chapters.title: Desegregating the Bowling Alley
chapters.summary: She tells the story of the SNCC athlete Stanley Tillman taking her to bowl at the only alley in the area, where the staff refused them shoes but could not deny them a lane, so they bowled in their socks until the police arrived and made them leave, her first game ever.

chapters.title: Making Sense of the Organizers
chapters.summary: Young describes coming to understand the young organizers through meetings in their homes and traveling to multi-day workshops at the Presbyterian village in Ferndale, wanting to be in everything while much of the community held back.

chapters.title: Where the Fearlessness Came From
chapters.summary: She traces her fearlessness to Lexa, a small self-sustaining community with its own post office, station, and businesses where, despite the demarcation, both sides of the track came together in a crisis, before age and family circumstance moved them to West Helena.

chapters.title: An Assault in the Snow Lake Field
chapters.summary: Young recounts being attacked by a group of young men on a cotton-picking trip to a Snow Lake field, an assault interrupted by the water boy that left her with an injury requiring surgery years later, an ordeal that hardened her resolve to leave the Delta.

chapters.title: The Storefront Petition, and Deciding to Leave
chapters.summary: She remembers the lonely work of gathering poll-tax-abolition signatures alone on a storefront porch, writing names for those who could only mark an X, and her decision to leave for Washington even while secretly engaged to Howard Himmelbaum.

chapters.title: Loving Howard Himmelbaum
chapters.summary: Young describes the secretive courtship with the white organizer Howard Himmelbaum, the few in the Black community who objected, her mother's open affection for him and her father's deeper fear, less of Howard than that his daughter would be hurt.

chapters.title: Marches, and Memphis
chapters.topic: On the Road With the Movement
chapters.summary: She recalls the marches and trips she took, sharing rooms with Stokely Carmichael in Memphis around the time Dr. King was killed, and the poll-tax focus that white residents never faced because the tax existed only to keep Black people from voting.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

chapters.title: Arrested for Asking for Food
chapters.summary: Young tells of returning to West Helena with the Black United Youth one hungry summer, asking a grocery manager for food, being refused, taking what she needed, and being jailed until the police recognized her father and let her go.

chapters.title: The Wider Delta, and Judge Hester's Threat
chapters.summary: She sketches the wider Arkansas effort in Gould, Pine Bluff, and Forrest City, the shootout that made people wary of Forrest City, the Helena judge who threatened the young engineer James Heel to leave town, and the Masons who quietly took protective action.

chapters.title: A Divided Community, and an Open Home
chapters.summary: Young describes a Black community divided over SNCC, with absentee pastors and the deacons and church mothers who controlled access, the freedom houses, and her own family's openness, which once cost an uncle his life but never stopped their helping.

chapters.title: Myrtle Glascoe, a New Big Sister
chapters.summary: She recalls Myrtle Glascoe arriving from Oakland in a Volkswagen to work the SNCC summer program, a fair-skinned Black woman who lived with the family and became the big sister Young had lost, and who carried her along on travels with her mother's blessing.

chapters.title: Atlanta, and the Tensions in SNCC
chapters.summary: Young remembers a week of constant meetings and strategizing in Atlanta and the SNCC tensions she heard but stayed out of, trusting they would pass, alongside her impressions of Bill Hansen and Ben Grinage and the privacy the organizers kept.

chapters.title: Marriage, and Living the Struggle
chapters.summary: She describes marrying Howard in 1967 and living the daily struggle of an interracial couple, refusing to hide who she was, balancing movement work with finishing high school, and the protection Jerry Casey and others gave the freedom house.

chapters.title: Nonviolence, and the Turn to Self-Defense
chapters.summary: Young recalls the questioning of nonviolence as Dr. King's presence drew danger, and how the Black Panther Party and the armed students of the Cornell University protest, one of them from Little Rock, grew from a determination to protect Black lives and leaders.

chapters.title: Key Punch, and Suing for the Job
chapters.summary: After marrying in New York she trained as a key-punch operator at the Opportunities Industrialization Center and sued employers who would not hire a qualified Black woman, winning class-action suits in Pulaski County against companies including AMF and National Old Line Life Insurance.

chapters.title: Fired From the Capitol for Her Marriage
chapters.summary: Young describes becoming the first Black office worker in the State Capitol administration, placed in the demanding Motor Vehicle Department, and being targeted for dismissal by a director who learned she was married to a white man and ordered her gone.

chapters.title: Rockefeller, and Winning the Job Back
chapters.summary: She recounts taking her case through attorney John W. Walker to Governor Winthrop Rockefeller, who fired the department head on the spot, the barefoot walk through hot asphalt that a reporter put in the paper, and the test she aced four times over to win the job back.

chapters.title: Black United Youth and the Night in Earle
chapters.summary: Young describes her years with the Black United Youth and the violent night in Earle when the schools were desegregated by closing the Black high school, state troopers beating people and one aiming a Magnum at her, and the armed friends waiting back at the house.

chapters.title: Bobby Brown, Bob Broadwater, and Electing Rockefeller
chapters.summary: She portrays Bobby Brown of the Black United Youth and the radio newsman Bob Broadwater, their push for student representation and a Black media perspective, the power struggles and Brown's anti-white stance against her own, and the negotiations that helped elect Rockefeller.

chapters.title: Protecting Dr. King, and a Verdict on the Late 60s
chapters.summary: Young explains that she went to Memphis not to hear Dr. King but to be eyes, ears, and protection, knowing guns would be aimed at him, and her hard verdict that the wrong people stood closest to him and that the change she wanted never came.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

chapters.title: What the Movement Did and Didn't Change
chapters.summary: She names economic change and equal access as her own goals, argues the struggle was about civil rights and the Constitution rather than measuring up to white people, and points to the drugs that came home from Vietnam and the execution of Troy Davis as signs of how far the country still fell short.

chapters.title: Reading the Obama Election
chapters.summary: Young describes the numbness she saw toward Barack Obama until after the primary, her offense at his being asked for a resume and a birth certificate, and her firm support for a qualified Black man whose election her parents and children had died for.

chapters.title: Faith and Free Speech
chapters.summary: She reflects on her Christian faith and her wish that the President governed more from it, and voices a strong conviction that freedom of speech must be protected even for words she considers offensive, a liberty she argues should be met with maturity rather than law.

chapters.title: Malcolm X Liberation University
chapters.summary: Young recounts her 1971 year at Malcolm X Liberation University in Durham, drawn partly by a need for acceptance after Black peers in West Helena had not embraced her, where she studied African ethnic groups and martial arts but still met people who could not see past color when her white husband visited.

chapters.title: Closing: Faith, Myrtle, and Worth Long
chapters.summary: In closing she thanks the Lord and Myrtle Glascoe for recognizing the field workers who gave all they had, thanks her interviewers and her employer for the time to come home, and remembers Worth Long as a gentle, free-spirited man who always visited her parents.

loc_video.caption: Carrie M. Young oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Little Rock, Arkansas, 2011 September 26
